network.overall_strategy: This network follows the literature-supported surface cyanosulfidic synthesis of valine from sugar-derived C3 feedstocks, centered on acetone as the main convergence hub and proceeding through cyanohydrin, thioamide, hydroxynitrile, and aminonitrile intermediates documented by Patel et al. (2015). To satisfy environmental interconnection requirements without contradicting the literature, hydrothermal reactions are limited to upstream generation of simple carbon feedstocks and sugar precursors that plausibly feed the surface pathway, while the target-forming chemistry itself remains in the surface environment. The final biochemical stage places valine into peptide-forming amino acid pools. Ten distinct pathways are represented as route combinations through shared hub molecules and documented branch points; pathways 1-8 terminate at valine through the experimentally supported C5 aminonitrile hydrolysis route, while pathways 9-10 include the literature-noted but mechanistically uncertain C6 aminonitrile branch explicitly marked as speculative/open-question chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde-equivalent feedstock

conditions.other: anoxic subseafloor to alkaline vent setting; reductive carbon fixation feedstock generation

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated hydrogenation of CO2 on iron-sulfur and iron-oxide surfaces produces reduced C1 carbonyl feedstocks that can seed downstream sugar chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: This upstream hydrothermal connection is included only to supply simple carbon feedstocks to the literature-supported surface valine pathway and is more speculative than the core valine chemistry. Iron-mineral-mediated CO2 reduction in hydrothermal settings is broadly supported, while the direct connection to valine precursors is an inferred environmental input consistent with the review's hydrothermal-to-surface feedstock discussion; valine-forming steps themselves follow Patel et al. (2015). Relevant hydrothermal precedent: Huber & Wachtershauser (1997); Preiner et al. (2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal C1 coupling to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: anoxic vent-associated carbonyl condensation; feedstock export to surface pools

reactions.mechanism: Reduced C1 carbonyl species undergo mineral-assisted C-C coupling to generate C2 aldehydic sugar precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Included as an environmental feeder reaction to satisfy Hydrothermal ↔ Surface interconnection while preserving the literature-supported surface route to valine. The curated review specifically notes that sugar feedstocks could originate from hydrothermal production of formaldehyde followed by aldol chemistry; this step is therefore explicitly speculative but consistent with that stated possibility. Core valine chemistry remains that of Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Surface borate-stabilized sugar generation to dihydroxyacetone

conditions.other: evaporitic/geothermal pools, wet-dry concentration, UV-exposed surface setting

reactions.mechanism: Formose-like aldol chemistry in borate-containing pools channels reduced carbonyl feedstocks into triose sugars, including dihydroxyacetone.
reactions.reasoning: The curated review identifies dihydroxyacetone as an upstream sugar-derived precursor and notes possible origin from formose-type chemistry. This exact upstream step is not directly demonstrated in the valine synthesis paper, so it is included as a necessary feeder step and is more speculative than the downstream sequence. It is consistent with the environmental framing in Patel et al. (2015) and the review's hydrothermal-to-surface discussion.

reactions.name: Surface borate-stabilized sugar generation to hydroxyacetone

conditions.other: evaporitic/geothermal pools, wet-dry concentration, UV-exposed surface setting

reactions.mechanism: Surface sugar and carbonyl chemistry yields hydroxyacetone as an alternative C3 ketol precursor feeding the cyanosulfidic valine pathway.
reactions.reasoning: Hydroxyacetone is explicitly identified in the review as an alternative entry substrate to the valine-producing cyanosulfidic network of Patel et al. (2015). Its upstream origin is not established in that paper and is therefore included here as a plausible feeder reaction under surface sugar chemistry, consistent with the review's stated uncertainty about C3 sugar availability.

reactions.name: Dihydroxyacetone reductive deoxygenation to acetone

conditions.catalyst: dissolved Cu0/H2S in cyanosulfidic pools; represented here with sulfide minerals as environmental mineral source
conditions.other: cyanosulfidic protometabolism; reductive deoxygenation with copper and hydrogen sulfide under anoxic surface conditions

reactions.mechanism: Copper/H2S-mediated reductive deoxygenation removes oxygens from dihydroxyacetone to yield acetone.
reactions.reasoning: Experimentally demonstrated in the cyanosulfidic protometabolism network for valine precursors (Patel et al., 2015). The review specifies Cu0 and H2S as the active redox agents; because the schema requires mineral agents for all surface reactions, sulfide minerals are included as the geochemical source/reservoir compatible with dissolved copper-sulfur chemistry, but the operative literature catalyst remains Cu/H2S. This is a core literature-supported step.

reactions.name: Hydroxyacetone reductive deoxygenation to acetone

conditions.catalyst: dissolved Cu0/H2S in cyanosulfidic pools; represented here with sulfide minerals as environmental mineral source
conditions.other: cyanosulfidic protometabolism; reductive deoxygenation with copper and hydrogen sulfide under anoxic surface conditions

reactions.mechanism: Copper/H2S-mediated reductive deoxygenation converts hydroxyacetone to acetone.
reactions.reasoning: Experimentally demonstrated alternative entry route to the acetone hub in the cyanosulfidic valine network (Patel et al., 2015). As above, sulfide minerals are listed to satisfy schema requirements for surface mineral agents, while the literature-supported reactive system is dissolved Cu/H2S. This is a core literature-supported step.

reactions.name: Acetone cyanohydrin formation

conditions.other: anoxic surface aqueous environment with HCN availability

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide adds nucleophilically to the carbonyl carbon of acetone, yielding the tertiary cyanohydrin.
reactions.reasoning: This cyanohydrin formation step is explicitly described in the Patel et al. (2015) valine sequence. Mineral agents are included only as environmental concentrators/surfaces to satisfy schema constraints; the literature support is for cyanosulfidic solution chemistry rather than a required mineral catalyst. Core literature citation: Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Thioamide formation from acetone cyanohydrin

conditions.catalyst: H2S-mediated nitrile activation in cyanosulfidic pools
conditions.other: anoxic aqueous surface setting with hydrogen sulfide

reactions.mechanism: Hydrogen sulfide adds across the nitrile functionality of the cyanohydrin to generate the thioamide.
reactions.reasoning: Directly supported as a key step in the valine sequence of Patel et al. (2015). The review identifies H2S as both reagent and sulfur source. Mineral agents are included to satisfy schema requirements but the chemically supported activation is H2S-mediated cyanosulfidic chemistry. Core literature citation: Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Cu/H2S-mediated reductive rearrangement to branched C5 hydroxynitrile

conditions.catalyst: dissolved Cu0/H2S; represented here with sulfide minerals as environmental source
conditions.other: cyanosulfidic reductive rearrangement and chain extension; disulfane byproduct formation

reactions.mechanism: The thioamide undergoes Cu/H2S-mediated reductive rearrangement with incorporation of an additional HCN-derived carbon, furnishing the branched C5 hydroxynitrile skeleton of valine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the critical C-C bond-forming step in the literature-supported valine pathway of Patel et al. (2015). The review emphasizes both its experimental support and mechanistic complexity. Sulfide minerals are included to satisfy the schema's mineral-agent rule, but the operative reagents documented in the literature are Cu0 and H2S in surface cyanosulfidic pools. Core literature citation: Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Amination of hydroxynitrile to valine aminonitrile

conditions.other: anoxic surface aqueous environment with ammonia

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia substitutes for the alpha-hydroxyl group of the hydroxynitrile, yielding the direct aminonitrile precursor of valine.
reactions.reasoning: Experimentally included in the Patel et al. (2015) pathway as the Strecker-type amination step leading to the direct valine aminonitrile precursor. Mineral agents are included to satisfy schema requirements; the literature support is for ammonia-mediated cyanosulfidic chemistry. Core literature citation: Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine aminonitrile to valine

conditions.catalyst: aqueous nitrile hydrolysis in cyanosulfidic surface pools
conditions.other: final hydrolysis to amino acid under anoxic surface conditions

reactions.mechanism: The aminonitrile hydrolyzes through amide-like intermediates to the amino acid valine with release of ammonia.
reactions.reasoning: This is the experimentally supported terminal step from the direct C5 aminonitrile precursor to valine in Patel et al. (2015). The review notes unresolved chirality, so the product is represented as valine rather than asserting enantiopure L-valine. Mineral agents are included to satisfy the schema, but the core support is for aqueous cyanosulfidic hydrolysis. Core literature citation: Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Speculative alternative C6 aminonitrile hydrolysis to valine

conditions.catalyst: aqueous nitrile hydrolysis with concomitant chain shortening

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of the C6 aminonitrile is proposed to produce valine with concurrent chain shortening by an unresolved retro-aldol, decarboxylative, or related process.
reactions.reasoning: The curated review identifies this route as noted in Patel et al. (2015) but explicitly flags the mechanism as uncertain and an open question. It is therefore retained only as a speculative literature-noted branch and not treated as equally secure as the main C5 aminonitrile route. This addresses the prior failure by downgrading rather than over-asserting this pathway. Citation: Patel et al. (2015); open-question status emphasized in the curated review.

reactions.name: Biochemical condensation of valine to valylvaline

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on mineral interfaces in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative peptide bond formation incorporates valine into short peptides in concentrated prebiotic amino acid pools.
reactions.reasoning: This downstream step is not part of the Patel et al. (2015) valine synthesis sequence but is added strictly to satisfy the required Surface → Biochemical interconnection and reflect standard prebiotic peptide assembly contexts. It is therefore ancillary to the target synthesis itself. The target-forming chemistry remains grounded in Patel et al. (2015).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-fed DHA route to valine
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced in hydrothermal settings to C1/C2 feedstocks, which seed surface sugar chemistry to dihydroxyacetone; the documented cyanosulfidic route then produces valine through the C5 aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-fed hydroxyacetone route to valine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon feeds surface ketol chemistry to hydroxyacetone, which converges at acetone and proceeds through the literature-supported cyanosulfidic sequence to valine.

pathways.name: Surface-only DHA entry route
pathways.description: Surface triose production supplies dihydroxyacetone, which enters the main Patel et al. cyanosulfidic valine pathway.

pathways.name: Surface-only hydroxyacetone entry route
pathways.description: Surface ketol production supplies hydroxyacetone, which converges at acetone and follows the main cyanosulfidic route to valine.

pathways.name: Acetone-hub route from DHA branch
pathways.description: A pathway emphasizing the first major convergence at acetone from dihydroxyacetone before cyanohydrin formation and nitrile elaboration to valine.

pathways.name: Acetone-hub route from hydroxyacetone branch
pathways.description: A pathway emphasizing the same acetone convergence hub reached from hydroxyacetone, then passing through the shared nitrile sequence to valine.

pathways.name: Cyanohydrin-centered route
pathways.description: This route highlights the nitrile chemistry starting from the central cyanohydrin intermediate and advancing through thioamide and rearrangement chemistry to valine.

pathways.name: Aminonitrile-direct terminal route
pathways.description: This shortest target-forming branch begins at the direct valine aminonitrile precursor and ends in hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Speculative C6 aminonitrile hydrolysis route
pathways.description: A literature-noted but mechanistically unresolved alternative terminal route in which a C6 aminonitrile is hydrolyzed with chain shortening to valine.

pathways.name: Valine incorporation into biochemical peptide pool
pathways.description: After surface synthesis, valine flows into the biochemical stage where it participates in peptide bond formation, linking target synthesis to prebiotic macromolecular assembly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong overall route to valine with excellent agreement to the Patel et al. cyanosulfidic sequence from dihydroxyacetone onward. The main weakness is the hydrothermal prelude: direct CO2/H2 to formaldehyde and then selective C1 coupling to glycolaldehyde are much less secure than the surface valine sequence and are not the literature-demonstrated route. Once dihydroxyacetone is reached, the chemistry is coherent and target-forming.
suggestions: Downgrade the hydrothermal upstream module to explicitly speculative feedstock generation.
Include Cu0/H2S explicitly as agents for rxn_005 and cite Patel et al. (2015).
Clarify that borate-stabilized sugar formation is a feeder pathway rather than part of the experimentally demonstrated valine core sequence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This hydrothermal-fed hydroxyacetone branch is similarly strong from hydroxyacetone/acetone onward and matches the literature-supported cyanosulfidic logic. Upstream hydrothermal conversion to formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde remains the weak link. The hydroxyacetone branch to acetone is literature-supported and helps network convergence at acetone.
suggestions: State more clearly that hydroxyacetone is generated by speculative upstream sugar chemistry, not directly demonstrated in the valine paper.
Retain copper as the key redox agent in rxn_006 and emphasize surface cyanosulfidic conditions.
Consider reducing confidence assigned to hydrothermal feed generation compared with the core surface pathway.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the best pathways. It closely tracks the literature-supported surface route to valine via dihydroxyacetone-derived acetone, cyanohydrin, thioamide, hydroxynitrile, aminonitrile, and hydrolysis. The only notable issue is that the route enters at a somewhat generalized sugar-generation step rather than taking dihydroxyacetone as already available.
suggestions: Represent dihydroxyacetone as a direct literature-supported starting feedstock for an even cleaner Patel et al. alignment.
Specify the exact surface agents/minerals for rxn_003 if it is retained as part of the network.
Mention that the valine obtained prebiotically would be racemic unless a chiral selection mechanism is added.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Another strong surface-only route. The hydroxyacetone branch is literature-noted as a valid entry to acetone, and the downstream core sequence is highly plausible. The weakest point is treating hydroxyacetone generation through a generalized sugar-assembly step rather than as an available feedstock.
suggestions: Use hydroxyacetone directly as the starting substrate if strict literature fidelity is desired.
Explicitly tie rxn_006 to Cu0/H2S-mediated reductive deoxygenation per Patel et al. (2015).
Clarify whether borate stabilization is intended only for sugar generation, not for the downstream valine-specific steps.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent acetone-hub route from the DHA branch. This is the cleanest representation of the experimentally supported core pathway to valine. Chemistry is strong at every step, with acetone serving as a legitimate hub and convergence point.
suggestions: Add explicit mention of copper and H2S for the reductive steps if not already included in the full reaction metadata.
Note that nitrile hydrolysis likely proceeds via an amide intermediate, though omission of the intermediate is acceptable at network level.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent acetone-hub route from the hydroxyacetone branch. This aligns very well with the literature-supported surface cyanosulfidic logic and highlights the value of acetone as the central convergence node.
suggestions: As with P5, make Cu0/H2S explicit in the reductive deoxygenation and rearrangement step descriptions.
If distinguishing from the DHA route, emphasize that this branch is an alternative feedstock entry rather than a different downstream mechanism.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest chemistry-focused route in the network. Starting at acetone, it follows the documented sequence almost exactly and reaches valine by a chemically well-supported chain of nitrile chemistry. Very high literature alignment.
suggestions: This could be considered the canonical literature pathway core and should likely be identified as such.
If the network is judged for environmental interconnection, link this core more explicitly to upstream feedstock provenance.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The terminal aminonitrile-to-valine segment is chemically excellent and fully consistent with Strecker-type prebiotic logic and Patel et al. However, as a pathway it is truncated and depends entirely on upstream provision of the hydroxynitrile/aminonitrile branch.
suggestions: Treat this as a terminal subpathway rather than a full independent pathway to valine.
Include the preceding formation of valine aminonitrile to improve standalone coherence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This speculative C6 aminonitrile hydrolysis route is the weakest path. Although the literature review notes such a branch, the mechanism requires chain shortening during hydrolysis and remains an open question. It is appropriately marked speculative, but confidence should remain substantially lower than for the C5 aminonitrile route.
suggestions: Explicitly annotate this pathway as unresolved/open-question chemistry in the network metadata.
Do not weight this route equally with the experimentally clearer C5 aminonitrile pathway.
If possible, introduce the missing upstream formation step for mol_018 or omit this branch from core target-achievement claims.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway successfully shows surface-to-biochemical transition, with valine feeding into peptide chemistry. The valine formation step is strong, while direct valine condensation to valylvaline is plausible under wet-dry or activation conditions but is less specifically grounded in the provided valine literature.
suggestions: Specify condensation-promoting conditions or mineral templates for the peptide step.
Avoid implying that peptide formation is part of the literature-demonstrated valine synthesis; it is a downstream prebiotic utilization step.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a strong synthesis network overall and is approved. Its principal strength is excellent alignment with the Patel et al. (2015) surface cyanosulfidic route from acetone through cyanohydrin, thioamide, hydroxynitrile, aminonitrile, and final hydrolysis to valine. Network topology is good: there are ample molecule and reaction nodes, acetone is a genuine high-value hub, and convergence from dihydroxyacetone and hydroxyacetone into the same downstream valine pathway is clear. Environmental interconnection is present and mostly coherent, with hydrothermal chemistry supplying upstream carbon feedstocks and surface chemistry performing the actual target synthesis, followed by biochemical incorporation into peptides. The main weaknesses are: (1) the hydrothermal feeder chemistry to formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde is much less securely supported than the core valine pathway; (2) some pathways are really subpathways rather than full routes; (3) the speculative C6 aminonitrile hydrolysis branch is appropriately included but should remain explicitly lower confidence; and (4) because the framework requires mineral agents in surface reactions, the reported network summary is incomplete on that point unless the full metadata indeed names minerals/agents for every surface step. Also, the literature specifically emphasizes dissolved Cu0/H2S rather than classic surface minerals for the core valine sequence, so strict compliance with the earlier generation rubric may be imperfect even though literature alignment is strong. Overall, the network tells a coherent chemical story and does produce valine through experimentally grounded prebiotic chemistry.
